g.data-narration: Welcome to Balanced Search Trees. We explore AVL, Red-Black, and B-Trees that guarantee efficient O(log n) operations.
Basic binary search trees can become unbalanced, degrading to O(n) complexity when data is sorted.
g.text: Regular BST with sorted data (1,2,3,4,5) becomes a linked list:

g.data-narration: Balanced search trees solve this by enforcing balance conditions, guaranteeing O(log n) for all operations.
AVL trees maintain balance by ensuring the balance factor (left height minus right height) is -1, 0, or +1 for every node.
g.text: Balance Factor = Height(Left Subtree) - Height(Right Subtree)
BF must be -1, 0, or +1 for every node

g.data-narration: When AVL balance is violated, we perform rotations: LL, RR, LR, and RL to restore balance while preserving BST property.
In an RR case (right-heavy with right-heavy child), a single left rotation restores balance by moving the child up.
In an LL case (left-heavy with left-heavy child), a single right rotation moves the left child up to become the root.
LR and RL are double rotations needed when the child has opposite balance sign. LR rotates left child left, then node right.
g.text: Double Rotations: LR and RL Cases

g.data-narration: Let's insert 10, 20, 30 into an empty AVL tree. After inserting 30, the tree becomes right-right unbalanced.
Red-Black trees maintain balance through five color properties: root black, leaves black, no red-red, equal black-height.
Red-Black insertion has three cases based on uncle color: recolor when uncle is red, rotate when uncle is black.
Let's insert 7, 3, 18, 10 into a Red-Black tree. When inserting 10, we encounter a red-red violation with uncle 3.
AVL trees are more strictly balanced (1.44*log n height) ideal for search-heavy apps. Red-Black allows 2*log n, better for writes.
